# Extended Case References — Lecture 9jCXHL9awLk

*Brief mentions of canonical cases that did not warrant a full `case_index.md` entry. Generated by reconciliation pass against the 1,676-cluster canon. Substantive treatments are in `case_index.md`.*

## Cases mentioned in passing

### Propeller shaft fatigue failure (boat/sport unit)
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Propeller shaft fatigue failure (boat/sport unit)"
- **Anchor:** `§2.p6`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "For pressure vessels, as I mentioned, they have their different ways of evaluating stress level, and that's why in their code they have a very high safety factor. But again it doesn't mean you can pressurize it to four times the nominal pressure, because when you get to a transient situation — a water hammer or anything — you can have a failure. Thermal expansion will also consume some of the capacity. Fatigue is another parameter. If you do a fatigue analysis for what your capacity is, then you can reduce your safety factor, because you just know more, you're less ignorant.

## §3...."

### Trailer design joint failure example
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Trailer design joint failure example"
- **Anchor:** `§11.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "You want to think about the joints very early on. A stupid example: I built a trailer that has all the upper sheet metal — the cover is aluminum — and my frame is galvanized steel. I can't really connect them easily together, I've got to put fasteners everywhere. If I did it again today, I'd probably do all aluminum. A little more expensive in material, but I wouldn't have that joining issue. When I started I wasn't sure I was going to put a cover, so it's kind of part of the process. When you're coming up with something new, a lot of things look like "oh yeah, we'll figure it out" — but..."
